---
title: "Unix Command-Line Workspace Setup for Data Science Researchers"
author: "Ronald 'Ryy' G. Thomas"
date: "2026-05-17"
categories: [shell, macos, dotfiles, git, workflow, setup]
description: "A new researcher arrives with three laptops and no
  shared configuration. This post builds the terminal layer,
  the shell layer, and the dotfiles repository that keeps all
  three machines consistent from day one."
image: "media/images/hero.jpg"
document-type: "blog"
draft: true
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
format:
  html:
    code-fold: false
    code-tools: false
  pdf:
    pdf-engine: xelatex
    monofont: 'DejaVu Sans Mono'
    geometry: margin=1in
    fontsize: 11pt
    toc: false
---

<!-- ============================================================================
AUTHOR PROVIDES — concrete inputs the blogger must supply

YAML FRONT MATTER
  [x] title           — filled in
  [x] date            — 2026-05-17
  [x] categories      — shell, macos, dotfiles, git, workflow, setup
  [x] description     — filled in
  [x] image           — placeholder; replace with generated hero.jpg
  [ ] draft: false    — flip when imagery in place and render verified

NARRATIVE INPUTS
  [x] Hook sentence   — three-laptop pain point
  [x] Pain point      — configuration divergence across machines
  [x] Motivations     — 5 bullets
  [x] Objectives      — 4 verifiable end states
  [x] What is [the workspace]? — layered model
  [x] Daily-workflow paragraph — command table
  [x] Things to Watch Out For — 7 gotchas
  [x] Lessons Learnt  — conceptual / technical / gotchas
  [x] Limitations     — 5 bullets
  [x] Opportunities   — 5 next-step extensions
  [x] Wrapping Up     — conclusion + main takeaways
  [x] See Also        — related posts + key resources

CONFIGURATION DELIVERABLES
  [x] install.sh      — analysis/configs/install.sh
  [x] Makefile        — analysis/configs/Makefile
  [x] .gitignore      — analysis/configs/gitignore
  [x] Verification commands
  [x] Uninstall / rollback steps

VERSION MATRIX
  [ ] OS + version tested
  [ ] Tool versions pinned
  [ ] Date of last verification

IMAGES (4 total: hero + 3 ambiance, all in media/images/)
  [ ] Hero image (80% width)
  [ ] Ambiance image 1 (after Objectives)
  [ ] Ambiance image 2 (after Configuration)
  [ ] Ambiance image 3 (before Lessons Learnt)
  [ ] media/images/README.md updated

CONTACT & METADATA
  [x] Author name in YAML matches site author
  [x] Social links in Let's Connect present
============================================================================ -->

![  ](media/images/hero.jpg){.img-fluid width=80% fig-align="center"}

*One set of configuration files, three consistent workstations: a
dotfiles repository turns months of accumulated environment knowledge
into a two-command bootstrap.*

# Introduction

Consider the situation that arises, perhaps more often than one
might expect, when a new researcher joins a data science and
biostatistics laboratory equipped with three fresh macOS laptops: a
primary workstation on campus, a machine for home analysis, and a
lighter travel laptop for conferences. On the first day all three
are indistinguishable. By the third week, however, one has acquired
a custom `.zshrc`, another has accumulated a set of shell aliases
whose provenance no one can quite reconstruct, and the third still
presents the default macOS prompt. Six months later, reproducing a
particular analysis command on the second machine requires either
an unusually reliable memory or something closer to archaeology.

The Unix command-line workspace does not maintain itself. Left to
accumulate without deliberate management, three machines will develop
three divergent configurations, and the researcher who built them
will be the only person with any clear picture of the differences.
We have found that the solution is not, in practice, a matter of
imposing more discipline on oneself; it is rather a question of
establishing the right architecture from the start. That architecture
is a version-controlled dotfiles repository, kept in git, that
treats each machine as a reproducible deployment of a single
authoritative source.

We shall describe that architecture here in three layers: the
terminal emulator, the shell, and the dotfiles repository that ties
both together. The presentation is addressed primarily to a
researcher arriving with fresh hardware and no existing
configuration, though the same procedure applies to anyone who has
accumulated an ad-hoc setup and wishes to consolidate it. The three
deliverables we shall present (an `install.sh` installer, a
`Makefile`, and a `.gitignore` template) are intended as generic
starting points; they merit adaptation to the specific tools and
conventions of one's own laboratory, not copying verbatim.

## Motivations

We have several reasons for approaching the problem this way. To
begin with, three laptops managed by memory alone will diverge
within weeks. The researcher who assembled them is, practically
speaking, the only person who can reason about the differences,
and that person is frequently unavailable at the moment the second
machine is needed.

Beyond simple divergence, reproducing a broken analysis environment
on a second machine is among the harder classes of reproducibility
failure one encounters in practice. The dependencies are invisible,
the state is implicit, and the only documentation is typically the
original researcher's recollection. A new-machine bootstrap that
demands several hours of manual configuration is, furthermore, a
recurring tax on every hardware refresh, contract renewal, and
collaborative handoff in a laboratory of any size.

We should also note that configuration files accumulated over years
of daily use contain hard-won knowledge: project navigation
shortcuts, editor settings, safety aliases, job-scheduling patterns.
Unfortunately, this knowledge has no other repository. Losing a
machine without version control means losing that knowledge entirely,
and the cumulative cost of such losses is not trivial.

Finally, the Unix tools that make a data science workflow genuinely
efficient, including fuzzy file finding, consistent vi-mode across
all command-line clients, per-project directory jumping, and
syntax-highlighted diffs, require deliberate configuration. The
default settings are functional; the configured environment is
qualitatively different in ways that compound over a working day.

## Objectives

We have four concrete goals. First, we will configure a modern
terminal emulator and shell on the primary machine, with settings
documented and placed under version control from the first session.
Second, we will create a `~/dotfiles` git repository outside any
cloud-synchronisation path, containing shell configuration, editor
settings, git configuration, system scripts, and a sensitive-files
inventory. Third, we will write and test an `install.sh` with
`--dry-run` support that bootstraps a fresh machine to the same
state as the primary in under thirty minutes. Fourth, and perhaps
most importantly, we will verify the bootstrap on the second machine
before the third is needed, so the repository becomes a tested
deployment artefact rather than a wishlist.

![  ](media/images/ambiance1.jpg){.img-fluid}

# What is a Unix Workspace?

A Unix command-line workspace is a layered system. Each layer has a
distinct responsibility, configures a distinct set of files, and
fails in a distinct way when misconfigured. Understanding the layers
is more useful than memorising any particular setting, because the
layers generalise across machines, operating-system versions, and
tool upgrades.

| Layer | Responsibility | Configured via |
|---|---|---|
| Terminal emulator | Window, fonts, GPU rendering, input | `kitty.conf` or `ghostty/config` |
| Shell | Commands, history, aliases, scripting | `.zshrc`, `.zshenv` |
| Editor | Text editing, language servers | `.vimrc` or `nvim/init.lua` |
| Version control | Diff, history, multi-machine sync | `.gitconfig`, `.gitignore_global` |
| Line editor | Vi-mode in R, Python, database clients | `.inputrc` |
| Dotfiles repository | Deploys all of the above reproducibly | `install.sh`, `Makefile` |

Think of the dotfiles repository as the source code for the
workspace. Just as application code can be cloned and built on a
fresh server, a dotfiles repository can be cloned and installed on
a fresh laptop. The analogy holds: the repository should live in
git, be tested before deployment, and have a clear migration path
for breaking changes.

# Prerequisites

This post targets macOS 13 (Ventura) or later on Apple Silicon or
Intel hardware. The shell configuration extends to Ubuntu 22.04+
with minor path adjustments.

Install the following before starting:

```bash
# Homebrew (if not yet installed)
/bin/bash -c \
  "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Homebrew/install/HEAD/install.sh)"

# Core tools
brew install git gh pipx

# Generate an SSH key for GitHub (if none exists)
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "your_email@university.edu"
# Then add the public key to your GitHub account settings
```

Confirm access before proceeding:

```bash
gh auth login     # follow the browser prompt
gh auth status    # should report 'Logged in to github.com'
```

Time budget: 2-3 hours for the initial setup on the primary
machine; 20-30 minutes per subsequent machine.

# Layer 1: Terminal Emulator

The terminal emulator draws the window, renders text using the GPU,
and forwards keystrokes to the shell. Two terminals are well suited
to a data science workflow on macOS.

**Kitty** (`brew install --cask kitty`) is GPU-accelerated with a
built-in graphics protocol (`kitten icat`) that renders matplotlib
and R plots inline. Configuration is a single plain-text file at
`~/.config/kitty/kitty.conf`.

**Ghostty** (`brew install --cask ghostty`) is a newer
GPU-accelerated terminal with sensible defaults and native macOS
integration. Configuration lives at `~/.config/ghostty/config`.
It requires less initial configuration than Kitty.

The choice is a matter of preference. The worked examples below use
Kitty because its configuration is more explicit and therefore more
instructive for a first setup.

## Core Kitty Settings

```sh
# ~/.config/kitty/kitty.conf

# Fonts
font_family      JetBrainsMono Nerd Font Mono
font_size        14.0

# Cursor
cursor_shape           block
cursor_blink_interval  0

# Clipboard: selecting text copies immediately
copy_on_select clipboard

# Splits and tabs
map cmd+d   launch --location=vsplit --cwd=current
map cmd+t   new_tab
map cmd+w   close_window
map cmd+shift+] next_tab
map cmd+shift+[ previous_tab

# macOS integration
macos_option_as_alt                  yes
macos_quit_when_last_window_closed   yes
```

Install the Nerd Font variant (required for prompt icons):

```bash
brew install --cask font-jetbrains-mono-nerd-font
```

The setting with the largest day-to-day effect is
`copy_on_select clipboard`: selecting any text with the mouse
copies it to the clipboard without a separate `Cmd+C` keystroke.

# Layer 2: Shell Configuration

## Environment Variables (.zshenv)

`.zshenv` is loaded by every shell instance, interactive or not.
Keep it minimal: only environment variables that every process
needs.

```sh
# ~/.zshenv
export DOTFILES="${DOTFILES:-$HOME/dotfiles}"
export PRJ_ROOT="${PRJ_ROOT:-$HOME/projects}"
export EDITOR="nvim"
export VISUAL="nvim"
```

`DOTFILES` and `PRJ_ROOT` are the two variables that all other
configuration and all project scripts should reference. Setting
them here, with defaults, means a fresh machine that has not yet
run `install.sh` still gets sensible fallback values.

## Shell Configuration (.zshrc)

### History

```sh
HISTFILE="$HOME/.zsh_history"
HISTSIZE=100000
SAVEHIST=100000
setopt SHARE_HISTORY HIST_IGNORE_DUPS
setopt INC_APPEND_HISTORY HIST_VERIFY
```

One hundred thousand lines is not excessive for research work.
Model training runs, pipeline executions, and database queries
generate commands worth recalling weeks later. `SHARE_HISTORY`
syncs history across all open terminal sessions in real time;
`INC_APPEND_HISTORY` writes each command immediately rather than
at session end, so a crashed terminal loses nothing.

### Project Navigation

```sh
cdpath=($PRJ_ROOT $PRJ_ROOT/.. $HOME)
setopt auto_cd
```

With `cdpath`, typing `cd myproject` from anywhere causes Zsh to
search `$PRJ_ROOT/myproject` automatically. Combined with
`auto_cd` (which permits typing a directory name without `cd`),
navigating between analysis projects becomes a single word rather
than a full path. The `$PRJ_ROOT` indirection is deliberate:
changing the project tree location requires editing one line in
`.zshenv`, not dozens of scripts.

### Git Branch in the Prompt

```sh
autoload -Uz vcs_info
precmd() { vcs_info }
zstyle ':vcs_info:git:*' formats '%b '

PROMPT='%F{cyan}%m%f %F{green}%*%f '
PROMPT+='%F{yellow}${${PWD:A}/$HOME/~}%f '
PROMPT+='%F{red}${vcs_info_msg_0_}%f$ '
```

A prompt that shows the current git branch prevents the most
common commit error in research projects: committing to the wrong
branch when switching between experiments. The `vcs_info` hook is
lightweight and adds no extra git processes.

### Modern CLI Replacements

Several tools have superseded the default Unix utilities for
interactive use. Install them in one step:

```bash
brew install eza bat fd ripgrep zoxide fzf git-delta lazygit \
             shellcheck
```

Wire them into Zsh with conditional guards:

```sh
# eza: ls with git awareness and tree mode
command -v eza > /dev/null && {
  alias ls='eza --group-directories-first'
  alias ll='eza -l --git --group-directories-first'
  alias la='eza -la --git --group-directories-first'
  alias lt='eza --tree --level=2 --git-ignore'
}

# bat: syntax-highlighted cat; paging off for pipeline safety
command -v bat > /dev/null && \
  alias cat='bat --paging=never --style=plain'

# delta: side-by-side pager for git diff
command -v delta > /dev/null && \
  export GIT_PAGER='delta'

# zoxide: frequency-weighted directory jumping
command -v zoxide > /dev/null && \
  eval "$(zoxide init zsh --cmd j)"

# fzf: fuzzy search, with ripgrep as file source
command -v rg > /dev/null && {
  export FZF_DEFAULT_COMMAND='rg --files --hidden'
  export FZF_DEFAULT_OPTS='-m --height 50% --border --reverse'
}
```

The conditional guard (`command -v tool > /dev/null &&`) is the
key portability decision: the same `.zshrc` deploys to a fresh
machine before tools are installed, skipping aliases gracefully
until the corresponding package is present.

### Fuzzy File Finders

Three functions cover the most common interactive search patterns
in a research compendium:

```sh
# find any file, cd to its directory
ff() {
  local file
  file=$(rg --files "${1:-.}" 2>/dev/null \
         | fzf --select-1 --exit-0)
  [[ -n "$file" ]] && cd "$(dirname "$file")"
}

# find an R/Rmd/qmd file, open in editor
rr() {
  local f
  f=$(rg --files 2>/dev/null | rg '\.(R|Rmd|qmd)$' | fzf)
  [[ -n "$f" ]] && $EDITOR "$f"
}

# find a PDF, open it
pp() {
  local f
  f=$(rg --files 2>/dev/null | rg '\.pdf$' | fzf)
  [[ -n "$f" ]] && open "$f"
}
```

### Readline Configuration (.inputrc)

Many command-line tools use the GNU Readline library for line
editing: R, Python's REPL, `psql`, and most database clients.
Without `.inputrc`, each of these uses Emacs-mode keybindings
while Zsh uses vi-mode, requiring the researcher to switch mental
models with every tool change. The fix:

```sh
# ~/.inputrc
set editing-mode vi
set show-mode-in-prompt on
set vi-ins-mode-string \1\e[6 q\2
set vi-cmd-mode-string \1\e[2 q\2
set completion-ignore-case on
set show-all-if-ambiguous on
set colored-stats on
"\e[A": history-search-backward
"\e[B": history-search-forward
```

The cursor shape changes between insert (thin bar) and command
(block) mode, providing a persistent visual indicator.

### Git Configuration (.gitconfig)

```ini
[init]
    defaultBranch = main

[core]
    editor = nvim
    excludesfile = ~/.gitignore_global
    pager = delta

[pull]
    rebase = false

[alias]
    st  = status --short
    lg  = log --oneline --graph --decorate -20
    co  = checkout
    br  = branch

[diff]
    colorMoved = default

[merge]
    conflictstyle = diff3

[credential "https://github.com"]
    helper =
    helper = !/opt/homebrew/bin/gh auth git-credential
```

The `gh auth git-credential` helper delegates all GitHub
authentication to the GitHub CLI. After `gh auth login` runs
once, no personal access tokens or SSH key passphrases are
needed for subsequent git operations.

# Layer 3: The Dotfiles Repository

With the configuration files defined, the next step is putting
them under version control so they can be deployed to all three
machines.

## Repository Structure

```
~/dotfiles/
├── README.md
├── install.sh           # deployment script
├── Makefile             # convenience targets
├── .gitignore           # blocks sensitive files
├── shell/
│   ├── zshrc
│   ├── zshenv
│   └── inputrc
├── git/
│   ├── gitconfig
│   └── gitignore_global
├── editors/
│   └── vimrc
├── config/
│   ├── kitty/
│   │   └── kitty.conf
│   └── ghostty/         # alternative terminal
├── bin/
│   └── <custom scripts>
├── launchd/             # macOS scheduled jobs
│   └── *.plist
├── packages/
│   ├── Brewfile
│   └── pipx-tools.txt
└── secrets/
    └── README.md        # inventory only; no secrets in git
```

Files are stored without leading dots (`zshrc`, not `.zshrc`).
The installation script adds the dot prefix when creating
symlinks in `$HOME`, making the mapping explicit and the
repository contents visible in standard directory listings.

## Write .gitignore First

The `.gitignore` must exist before the first `git add`. One
commit with a credential in history requires a full
`git filter-repo` rewrite and rotation of every exposed
credential. The template is at `analysis/configs/gitignore`.
Key patterns:

```gitignore
# Sensitive: never commit
secrets/*
!secrets/README.md
**/.env
**/.env.local
**/credentials*
**/*.pem
**/*.key
**/id_rsa*
**/id_ed25519*

# Per-machine state
*.backup.*
.DS_Store
.zsh_history
.zcompdump*

# Editor plugin directories (regenerated at install time)
config/vim/bundle/
config/vim/plugged/
config/nvim/plugged/
config/nvim/coc/
```

## Initialise and Push

```bash
cd ~/dotfiles
git init
git add .
git status       # review; confirm no sensitive files present
git commit -m 'initial dotfiles import'
gh repo create yourhandle/dotfiles --private --source=. --push
```

The repository must be private. Even a `.gitconfig` without
credentials reveals preferred tooling, organisational paths, and
workflow patterns that belong to the researcher, not the public.

![  ](media/images/ambiance2.jpg){.img-fluid}

## install.sh

The installer creates symlinks from `~/dotfiles` into `$HOME`,
handles launchd plist parameterisation, and installs packages
via Homebrew and pipx. The full source is at
`analysis/configs/install.sh`.

Key design decisions:

**`--dry-run` flag.** Every action is printed without execution
when `--dry-run` is passed. Always run the dry-run on a new
machine before the real install; it surfaces every hardcoded
assumption that does not hold on the new hardware.

**Timestamped backups.** Before creating a symlink, existing
files are moved to `filename.backup.TIMESTAMP`. The installer
is idempotent: it can be re-run on an already-deployed machine
without destroying local changes.

**Per-file symlinks for `~/bin`.** The installer creates one
symlink per script (`~/bin/script -> ~/dotfiles/bin/script`)
rather than a top-level link (`~/bin -> ~/dotfiles/bin`). A
top-level symlink works on one machine but dangles silently on
any machine with a different absolute path.

**launchd `__USER__` substitution.** macOS `launchd` does not
expand `~` in plist files. The installer substitutes the
`__USER__` placeholder with `$USER` when writing plist files
to `~/Library/LaunchAgents/`, keeping the source templates
machine-agnostic.

```bash
./install.sh --dry-run   # review all planned actions
./install.sh             # execute
```

## Makefile

The Makefile provides a stable interface for common operations.
The full source is at `analysis/configs/Makefile`.

| Target | Action |
|---|---|
| `make install` | create symlinks; install Homebrew and pipx packages |
| `make update` | `git pull` then re-run `install.sh` |
| `make lint` | `shellcheck` on `bin/`; `zsh -n` on shell files |
| `make test` | dry-run + lint |
| `make help` | list all targets |

## Sensitive Files Inventory

Before the first `git add`, audit `$HOME` for sensitive material:

```
~/.aws/           AWS credentials
~/.ssh/           SSH private keys
~/.gnupg/         GPG private keys
~/.docker/        registry authentication tokens
~/.npmrc          npm registry token
~/.config/rclone/ OAuth tokens for cloud storage
~/.env            project secrets sourced into shell
~/.password-store pass encrypted credential store
```

None of these belong in git. Add each to `.gitignore` before
the first commit. Create `secrets/README.md` listing each item
and its restore mechanism (1Password, pass, manual transfer via
an encrypted drive). On a new machine, restore secrets manually
after `install.sh` runs; the installer prints a reminder.

# Bootstrapping Three Machines

With the dotfiles repository on GitHub, bootstrapping the second
and third machines is a two-command operation:

```bash
# On the new machine, after installing Homebrew and git:
git clone git@github.com:yourhandle/dotfiles ~/dotfiles
cd ~/dotfiles && ./install.sh
```

Then manually restore sensitive files from secure storage. The
installer prints a checklist derived from `secrets/README.md`.

The recommended sequence for a three-machine lab setup:

1. **Machine 1 (primary):** complete the full setup in this
   post; commit and push to GitHub.
2. **Machine 2:** clone, run `--dry-run`, inspect the output,
   then execute. Fix any failures in the repository (they
   almost always reflect an assumption invisible on Machine 1).
3. **Machine 3:** clone and run. If Machine 2 succeeded without
   fixes, Machine 3 should be silent.

The second bootstrap always surfaces the assumptions hidden in
the first. This is the most valuable property of the three-machine
requirement: it forces the repository into a state where a fresh
environment is a first-class test case, not an afterthought.

# Verification

After each bootstrap, verify the key invariants:

```bash
# 1. Symlinks resolve correctly
ls -la ~/.zshrc ~/.gitconfig ~/.config/kitty

# 2. Environment variables are set
echo "$DOTFILES"
echo "$PRJ_ROOT"

# 3. CLI tools are available
for t in eza bat fd rg fzf zoxide delta lazygit; do
  command -v "$t" > /dev/null \
    && echo "$t: ok" || echo "$t: missing"
done

# 4. Git prompt shows branches in a repo
cd /path/to/any/git/repo
# Branch name should appear in prompt in red

# 5. Readline vi-mode works in R
R --quiet
# Press Escape; cursor should change to a block
# Press k to move up in history
# Press i to return to insert mode

# 6. Dry-run and lint pass cleanly
cd ~/dotfiles && make test
```

A clean `make test` with no warnings confirms the repository is
in a deployable state and can be trusted for Machine 3.

# Daily Workflow

After setup, the dotfiles repository is a standard git project.

| Command | Action |
|---|---|
| `cd ~/dotfiles && git diff` | Review uncommitted config changes |
| `make test` | Lint and dry-run before committing |
| `make install` | Re-link after adding a new config file |
| `make update` | Pull from GitHub and re-run install |
| `brew bundle dump --force` | Refresh Brewfile after new installs |
| `j <partial>` | Jump to a frequently visited directory |
| `ll` / `lt` | eza long listing / tree view |
| `rr` | Fuzzy-find and open an R/Rmd/qmd file |
| `pp` | Fuzzy-find and open a PDF |
| `ff` | Fuzzy-find any file, cd to its directory |

The most useful habit is running `make update` on each machine
at the start of every work session: it takes ten seconds and
ensures that a setting committed on the primary machine yesterday
is available everywhere today.

# Things to Watch Out For

1. **Write `.gitignore` before `git init`, not after.** If any
   sensitive file is committed even once, removing it requires
   a full `git filter-repo` rewrite and rotation of every
   exposed credential. The template in `analysis/configs/gitignore`
   covers the standard sensitive categories; review it before
   running `git add .`.

2. **Do not place the dotfiles repository inside Dropbox,
   iCloud, or Google Drive.** Git writes to `.git/index`,
   `.git/HEAD`, and ref files on every operation. A cloud
   provider racing to sync those writes can corrupt the index
   or truncate files to zero bytes. The repository must be
   local to each machine; git with a GitHub remote is the
   synchronisation mechanism.

3. **Run `install.sh --dry-run` on the second machine before
   the real run.** The first dry-run on a fresh environment
   surfaces every hardcoded assumption invisible on the primary
   machine. Look especially for paths that reference a directory
   that does not exist on the new hardware.

4. **Do not track editor plugin directories.** The directories
   where plugin managers deposit downloaded code
   (`~/.config/vim/bundle/`, `~/.config/nvim/plugged/`) are
   large, platform-specific, and regenerated automatically on
   first launch. Track only the plugin manifest (the `vimrc`
   `Plug` lines or `nvim/lua/plugins.lua`); add the download
   directories to `.gitignore` before the first commit.

5. **Per-file symlinks in `~/bin`, not a top-level directory
   link.** A `~/bin -> ~/dotfiles/bin` symlink appears to work
   but dangles silently on any machine where the absolute path
   differs. The installer creates one symlink per script; this
   is more verbose but correct on every machine.

6. **The launchd `__USER__` substitution writes to
   `~/Library/LaunchAgents/`, not back to the repository.** Edit
   the source template in `~/dotfiles/launchd/` and re-run the
   installer. Never edit the substituted plist in
   `~/Library/LaunchAgents/` directly; it is overwritten on the
   next `install.sh` run.

7. **`SHARE_HISTORY` shares across all terminal sessions in real
   time.** A command typed in one tab is immediately available in
   all others. The side effect is interleaved history from
   concurrent sessions. If this is disorienting, replace
   `SHARE_HISTORY` with `INC_APPEND_HISTORY` alone; history is
   then shared across sessions only after each session ends.

# Uninstall / Rollback

To remove the dotfiles installation and restore the previous
state:

```bash
# 1. Reverse symlinks (restore .backup.TIMESTAMP files)
for f in ~/.zshrc ~/.zshenv ~/.gitconfig \
          ~/.gitignore_global ~/.inputrc; do
  backup=$(ls "${f}.backup."* 2>/dev/null | tail -1)
  if [ -L "$f" ] && [ -n "$backup" ]; then
    rm "$f" && mv "$backup" "$f"
  fi
done

# 2. Remove per-file ~/bin symlinks
find ~/bin -maxdepth 1 -type l -delete

# 3. Unload and remove launchd agents
launchctl bootout gui/$UID \
  ~/Library/LaunchAgents/local.*.plist 2>/dev/null
rm -f ~/Library/LaunchAgents/local.*.plist

# 4. Remove ~/dotfiles (optional)
rm -rf ~/dotfiles
```

If no `.backup.TIMESTAMP` files exist (first-run on a machine
with no prior dotfiles), step 1 leaves empty broken symlinks
that must be removed manually. The `--dry-run` flag shows
exactly which files would be backed up before the real run.

![  ](media/images/ambiance3.jpg){.img-fluid}

# What Did We Learn?

## Lessons Learnt

**Conceptual Understanding:**

- The workspace is a layered system. The terminal emulator,
  shell, editor, line editor, and dotfiles repository are
  independent layers with independent configuration files and
  independent failure modes. Treating them as a coherent system
  from the start produces a qualitatively different working
  environment.
- Cloud sync and git are not substitutes. Cloud sync provides
  availability across machines; git provides history, diff,
  rollback, and the ability to deploy a specific state to a
  specific machine. The dotfiles repository needs git.
- The second bootstrap is more informative than the first.
  Setting up the primary machine is configuration; setting up
  the second machine is testing. Assumptions invisible on the
  primary machine surface immediately on the second.
- A sensitive-files inventory written before the first commit
  is a one-time investment that prevents a class of irreversible
  errors. Credential rotation after an accidental git commit is
  a half-day minimum.

**Technical Skills:**

- The `link_file` pattern (back up with a timestamp, then create
  a symlink) makes `install.sh` idempotent: it can be re-run on
  an already-deployed machine without destroying local changes.
- The `--dry-run` flag is low-cost to implement and essential for
  validating any installer before committing to a production
  machine. Ten seconds of review prevents an afternoon of
  recovery.
- Conditional tool guards (`command -v tool > /dev/null &&`) in
  `.zshrc` allow the same configuration to deploy before tools
  are installed and to a fully-configured machine without errors.
- `$OSTYPE` detection (`case "$OSTYPE" in darwin*) ... linux*)
  ... esac`) allows a single `.zshrc` to configure different PATH
  and alias settings for macOS and Linux without forking the
  repository.

**Gotchas and Pitfalls:**

- The order of operations matters: `.gitignore` before `git init`,
  `git status` review before `git add`, dry-run before the real
  run. Each shortcut creates a harder problem to undo.
- Editor plugin directories in `~/.config/vim/` or
  `~/.config/nvim/` are large, platform-specific, and silently
  break cross-machine deploys if tracked in git. The rule: if
  a directory is regenerated by running a tool, it is not user
  configuration.
- `delta` as a git pager requires both `export GIT_PAGER='delta'`
  in `.zshrc` and `[core] pager = delta` in `.gitconfig`. Either
  alone is insufficient.

## Limitations

- This configuration is tested on macOS 15.4 (Sequoia) with
  Apple Silicon and on Ubuntu 22.04. Other distributions, NixOS,
  and enterprise-managed workstations may require adjustments to
  PATH, plugin paths, and package names.
- The `install.sh` deploys to `$HOME` only. System-level
  configuration (`/etc/`, system-wide launchd daemons) is out of
  scope.
- The launchd plist handling is macOS-only. A Linux equivalent
  using systemd user units would require a separate code path.
- The secrets workflow is manual: the installer prints a reminder
  but provides no automation for restoring `~/.ssh`, `~/.aws`,
  or `~/.gnupg` on a new machine.
- No automated CI is applied. A GitHub Actions workflow that runs
  `install.sh --dry-run` on macOS and Ubuntu after every push
  would catch cross-platform breakage before a laptop bootstrap.

## Opportunities for Improvement

1. Add a `bootstrap.sh` that installs Homebrew and git before
   cloning, making the first command on a fresh machine a single
   `curl | bash` invocation (review the script content before
   running it).
2. Add a GitHub Actions workflow that runs `install.sh --dry-run`
   and `make lint` on macOS and Ubuntu runners after every push.
3. Write a systemd user-unit equivalent of the launchd plist
   section, conditioned on `linux*` in `$OSTYPE`, so the
   repository serves Linux development servers without a separate
   maintenance path.
4. Evaluate `git-crypt` for encrypting a small set of
   near-sensitive configs (rclone config, mbsyncrc) so they can
   live in the repository without manual scrubbing before each
   commit.
5. Complete the editor plugin manifest and add a post-install
   hook that runs `:PlugInstall` in headless vim, making the
   editor ready without manual intervention on a new machine.

# Wrapping Up

The three-laptop problem is a microcosm of the reproducibility
problem that motivates most of the Unix best-practice literature.
A workflow that exists only in a researcher's memory is not
reproducible: it cannot be audited, handed off, or recovered from
hardware failure. A workflow that exists in a version-controlled
repository is auditable, deployable, and recoverable.

The investment described in this post, a few hours on day one,
pays every time a machine is refreshed, every time a new
collaborator joins the lab, and every time the primary laptop
needs repair. The second and third machines are not
afterthoughts; they are the test suite that validates the
configuration.

**Main takeaways:**

- A dotfiles repository is the source code for the workspace.
  It should live in git, be tested before deployment, and have
  a clear migration path for breaking changes.
- Cloud sync is not version control. A dotfiles repository inside
  Dropbox or iCloud has no history, no diff, no rollback, and is
  subject to sync-race corruption. The repository must be local;
  GitHub is the remote.
- Write `.gitignore` before `git init`. This is a prerequisite,
  not a best practice. One sensitive commit requires a full
  rewrite and credential rotation.
- Test on the second machine before needing the third. The second
  bootstrap surfaces every assumption invisible on the primary
  machine. The time to find those assumptions is before the third
  machine is needed, not during.
- The conditional guard pattern in `.zshrc` is the key to
  portability: a configuration that degrades gracefully before
  tools are installed can be deployed on day one and improved
  incrementally.

# See Also

**Related posts on this site:**

- [Migrating Off Dropbox: Beyond Dotfiles](https://focusonr.org/posts/64-migrating-off-dropbox/)
  (post 64): the broader context -- project content,
  append-only history files, and the three-layer framework
  for workflow independence from cloud sync.
- [Multi-Laptop macOS Bootstrap](https://focusonr.org/posts/65-multi-laptop-bootstrap/)
  (post 65): a deeper audit of three specific blockers
  encountered when executing the dotfiles migration on a
  real workflow.
- [Setting Up pass, the Unix Password Manager](https://focusonr.org/posts/66-unix-pass-setup/)
  (post 66): GPG key generation, pass initialisation, and
  migration of credentials out of cloud sync.
- [Multi-Laptop Security: Hardening the Bootstrap](https://focusonr.org/posts/67-multi-laptop-security/)
  (post 67): a security audit of the infrastructure built
  in this post -- FileVault, GPG subkey architecture,
  per-machine SSH keys.
- [Modern CLI Replacements](https://focusonr.org/posts/53-modern-cli-replacements/)
  (post 53): the full reference for `eza`, `bat`, `fd`,
  `zoxide`, `delta`, and `lazygit`.
- [Secrets Management for Data Scientists](https://focusonr.org/posts/55-secrets-management/)
  (post 55): the three-tier credentials scheme that extends
  the sensitive-files inventory introduced here.

**Key resources:**

- [Dotfiles community guide](https://dotfiles.github.io/):
  curated showcase of community dotfiles repositories
- [Atlassian dotfiles guide](https://www.atlassian.com/git/tutorials/dotfiles):
  alternative bare-repository approach without `install.sh`
- [shellcheck](https://www.shellcheck.net/): static analysis
  for bash scripts; used by `make lint`
- [Zsh documentation](https://zsh.sourceforge.io/Doc/):
  official manual for options, completion, and scripting
- [launchd.info](https://www.launchd.info/): comprehensive
  reference for macOS launchd plist format
- [git-filter-repo](https://github.com/newren/git-filter-repo):
  the correct tool for removing sensitive data from git history

# Reproducibility

**Tested configuration:**

| Component        | Version     |
|------------------|-------------|
| Operating system | macOS 15.4  |
| zsh              | 5.9         |
| git              | 2.45.x      |
| Homebrew         | 4.3.x       |
| Kitty            | 0.36.x      |
| eza              | 0.18.x      |
| bat              | 0.24.x      |
| ripgrep          | 14.x        |
| fzf              | 0.54.x      |
| zoxide           | 0.9.x       |
| delta            | 0.17.x      |
| shellcheck       | 0.10.x      |
| Last verified    | [pending]   |

**Configuration deliverables:**

- `analysis/configs/install.sh` -- full installer source
- `analysis/configs/Makefile` -- build targets
- `analysis/configs/gitignore` -- `.gitignore` template

**To reproduce on a new machine:**

```bash
git clone git@github.com:yourhandle/dotfiles ~/dotfiles
cd ~/dotfiles
make test       # dry-run + lint
make install    # deploy symlinks and packages
```

---

*Rendered on 2026-05-17 at 16:18 PDT.*<br>
*Source: ~/prj/qblog/posts/68-unix-workspace-setup/unix-workspace-setup/analysis/report/index.qmd*

---

# Let's Connect

I would enjoy hearing from you if:

- You are setting up a research computing environment and have
  questions about adapting this configuration to your toolchain.
- You use a different secrets mechanism or sync strategy and want
  to compare notes.
- You have extended the launchd section to work with systemd on
  Linux and are willing to share the approach.
- You spot an error or a better approach to any of the code here.
- You just want to say hello and connect.

- **GitHub:** [rgt47](https://github.com/rgt47)
- **LinkedIn:** [Ronald Glenn Thomas](https://linkedin.com/in/rgthomaslab)
- **Email:** rgthomas47@gmail.com

---

<!-- ============================================================================
PRE-PUBLISH QA CHECKLIST: verify each item BEFORE setting draft: false

[ ] YAML
    [ ] title, date, categories, description, image filled in
    [ ] document-type: 'blog'
    [ ] draft: false

[ ] Narrative complete (no [bracketed] placeholders remaining)
    [ ] grep '\[' index.qmd returns only legitimate code/links

[ ] Configuration artifacts present and tested
    [ ] install.sh at analysis/configs/install.sh
    [ ] Makefile at analysis/configs/Makefile
    [ ] .gitignore template at analysis/configs/gitignore
    [ ] install.sh tested with --dry-run on primary machine
    [ ] install.sh tested on a second machine or fresh user account
    [ ] Verification commands produce the documented output

[ ] Things to Watch Out For
    [ ] At least 5 gotchas, each with symptom and fix

[ ] Visual design
    [ ] 1 hero image (width=80%, fig-align=center)
    [ ] 3 ambiance images (.img-fluid only, no width attribute)
    [ ] Captions describe actual images (not placeholder text)
    [ ] media/images/README.md attributes every image

[ ] Content quality
    [ ] Zero emoji
    [ ] Zero em dashes
    [ ] Single quotes in prose
    [ ] 78-character line wrap throughout

[ ] Reproducibility
    [ ] Version matrix 'Last verified' field filled in
    [ ] Config files committed under analysis/configs/

[ ] Render verification
    [ ] quarto render produces clean HTML with no warnings
    [ ] PDF renders without overflow or missing images
    [ ] Hero image displays in /blog/ listing card
    [ ] Internal links resolve
============================================================================ -->
